story.title: The School Newspaper
story.description: Jade and Sam run Linton School's student newspaper. From reporting on a science-fair discovery to interviewing teachers and tracking down sources, they learn that good journalism means asking the right questions — and reporting the answers honestly.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: a scientific test carried out under controlled conditions to discover or prove something
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The crystal experiment had been carried out by a Year Eight student called Rosa.

vocabulary.definition: facts or information that prove something is true or help to explain something
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Rosa had kept careful evidence of her process by photographing every stage.

vocabulary.definition: a room or building equipped with special tools and materials used for scientific experiments (often shortened to 'lab')
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The winning headline read: 'Year Eight Student Grows Perfect Crystals in School Laboratory.'

vocabulary.definition: correct in every detail, without any mistakes or errors
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Every detail in the newspaper article had to be accurate before it was published.

vocabulary.definition: the title of a newspaper article, printed in large letters at the top to catch the reader's attention
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The headline was changed three times before Jade and Sam agreed on one.

vocabulary.definition: to prepare a piece of writing and make it available for the public to read
vocabulary.exampleSentence: The school newspaper was published every two weeks, and today was deadline day.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use the passive when we want to focus on what happened rather than who did it. We form the passive with 'be' + past participle. In the present simple, we use 'is/are + past participle'. In the past simple, we use 'was/were + past participle'. We can add 'by + agent' to say who or what performed the action, but we often leave this out when the agent is unknown or unimportant. Passive forms with modals use 'modal + be + past participle'.
examples: The newspaper is published every two weeks. (present simple passive — no agent)
That article was written by Jade, and the photograph was taken by Sam. (past simple passive + by-agent)
Rosa's entry was judged by the science teachers and awarded first place. (past simple passive)
Mistakes cannot be published — everything must be checked first. (passive with modal)
The article will be reviewed by Ms Chen before it is sent to the printer. (future passive)

content.text: Jade arrived at the newspaper room at half past eight on Monday morning. The room was small but purposeful — two computers were switched on, and a stack of last week's edition had been left on the table near the door. Jade picked one up and read the headline. "Science Fair Crowns New Champion," it said. She smiled. That article had been written by her, and the photograph had been taken by Sam. The newspaper was published every two weeks, and today was deadline day for the next issue.

Sam came in a few minutes later, pulling off his coat. "Did you see the comments?" he asked. "People loved the crystal experiment story." Jade nodded. The experiment had been carried out by a Year Eight student called Rosa, who had grown crystals using salt, water, and controlled heat. The results had been carefully photographed and displayed on a poster at the fair. When Rosa's entry was judged by the science teachers, it was awarded first place. "We need a follow-up piece," Jade said. "More detail about how the experiment was actually done."

Sam opened his notebook. He had spoken to Rosa the previous afternoon. "She said the crystals were grown over three days," he said. "The water was heated to sixty degrees, and the salt was dissolved slowly. Then the mixture was left to cool, and the crystals formed as the water evaporated." Jade typed as he read. Every detail had to be accurate — the newspaper was read by students, parents, and teachers, and mistakes could not be published. "Has the process been tested again?" Jade asked. "She confirmed it can be repeated with other minerals too," Sam replied. He had kept careful evidence of every answer.

While Jade edited the text, Sam selected the photographs. Three pictures had been taken at the fair, but only one was sharp and well-lit. "This one should be used," Sam said, pointing at the screen. "The others are too dark." Jade agreed. The photograph showed Rosa holding a glass jar in which a cluster of white crystals had been formed. The jar had been placed against a white cloth so the details could be seen clearly. "Write a caption," Jade said. "Explain what is shown in the photograph."

By ten o'clock, the article was almost finished. The headline had been changed three times before they settled on: "Year Eight Student Grows Perfect Crystals in School Laboratory." Jade read it aloud. "Does that sound right?" she asked. "It sounds great," Sam said. The article would be reviewed by Ms Chen, their teacher supervisor, before it was sent to the printer. Everything in the newspaper was checked before it was published — that was the rule, and it had never been broken. Jade saved the file and leaned back in her chair. Another deadline met.

comprehension.question: Who wrote the article about the science fair that appeared in last week's edition?
options: Sam wrote it, and Jade took the photograph
Jade wrote it, and Sam took the photograph
Rosa wrote it with help from Jade
Ms Chen wrote it and Sam edited it
comprehension.correctAnswer: Jade wrote it, and Sam took the photograph
comprehension.explanation: The text says: 'That article had been written by her, and the photograph had been taken by Sam.' 'Her' refers to Jade.

comprehension.question: How were Rosa's crystals formed?
options: Salt was dissolved in cold water and the mixture was left to freeze overnight
Water was heated and salt was dissolved, then the mixture was left to cool and the crystals formed as the water evaporated
Crystals were grown under a lamp over the course of one week
Chemicals were mixed in a laboratory with special equipment
comprehension.correctAnswer: Water was heated and salt was dissolved, then the mixture was left to cool and the crystals formed as the water evaporated
comprehension.explanation: Sam explains Rosa's method: the water was heated to sixty degrees, salt was dissolved, the mixture was left to cool, and crystals formed as the water evaporated.

comprehension.question: Why does Jade say that every detail in the article must be accurate?
options: Because Ms Chen will give them a grade based on the quality of the writing
Because Rosa will correct any mistakes herself before publication
Because the newspaper is read by students, parents, and teachers and mistakes cannot be published
Because the article is being entered into a national journalism competition
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the newspaper is read by students, parents, and teachers and mistakes cannot be published
comprehension.explanation: The text says: 'Every detail had to be accurate — the newspaper was read by students, parents, and teachers, and mistakes could not be published.'

comprehension.question: What will happen to the article before it is sent to the printer?
options: It will be read by all the science teachers who judged the fair
Rosa will be asked to approve the final version
It will be reviewed by Ms Chen, the teacher supervisor
Sam will translate it into a shorter version for the school website
comprehension.correctAnswer: It will be reviewed by Ms Chen, the teacher supervisor
comprehension.explanation: The text says: 'The article would be reviewed by Ms Chen, their teacher supervisor, before it was sent to the printer.'

comprehension.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about how Jade and Sam approach their work?
options: They find the work stressful and are relieved when each issue is finished
They are proud of their work and take their responsibility to publish accurate content seriously
They prefer writing about science to writing about other topics
They disagree often about headlines but always agree on photographs
comprehension.correctAnswer: They are proud of their work and take their responsibility to publish accurate content seriously
comprehension.explanation: The rule that 'everything was checked before it was published — and had never been broken' shows they take accuracy seriously. Jade's smile and 'another deadline met' shows quiet pride in their work.

vocabulary.definition: a formal conversation in which someone is asked questions, usually to gather information for a report or article
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Jade had interviewed Mr Davies, the head of science, the previous afternoon.

vocabulary.definition: a person or document that provides information used in a report or article
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Ms Chen asked whether the article included enough quotes from both sources.

vocabulary.definition: the exact words that someone said, used in a written or spoken report
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Jade wanted to include a final quote from Rosa at the end of the article.

vocabulary.definition: one complete issue of a newspaper or magazine, published at a particular time
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Ms Chen reminded them that the deadline for the current edition was four o'clock.

vocabulary.definition: the time or date by which a piece of work must be finished
vocabulary.exampleSentence: When a deadline was missed, nothing was printed — that was the rule.

vocabulary.definition: to state that something is definitely true or to make a plan or arrangement certain
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Rosa had confirmed that her experiment could be repeated using other minerals.

grammarFocus.explanation: When we report what someone said, we usually shift the tenses back: present simple becomes past simple, past simple becomes past perfect, and will becomes would. We use 'said (that)' or 'told + object + (that)' for statements. For reported questions, we use 'asked if/whether' for Yes/No questions and 'asked + question word' for Wh- questions. The word order in reported questions is the same as in a normal sentence (subject + verb), not the inverted order of a direct question.
examples: Rosa said that she had become interested in crystals after watching a video online. (present → past; past → past perfect)
She told Sam that she had repeated the experiment four times. (told + object + that)
He explained that the fair gave students a chance to investigate their own questions. (explain + that)
Ms Chen asked whether they had included enough quotes from both sources. (asked whether — reported Yes/No question)
Rosa confirmed that the experiment could be repeated at home. (modal backshift: can → could)

content.text: The following morning, Jade spread her notes across the desk and looked at Sam. "I have everything Rosa told us," she said. "And I got some good quotes from Mr Davies as well." Sam opened his notebook. Their next article was about the importance of the science fair in the school's learning programme. It was a bigger piece than the crystal story — longer, and with more sources. "Let's start with Rosa," Jade said. "Read out what she said to you."

Sam read carefully from his notes. Rosa had told him that she had become interested in crystals after watching a documentary online. She had also said that she had repeated the experiment four times before she was satisfied with the results. "She mentioned that the most difficult part was controlling the temperature," Sam added. "She said it had to be kept constant throughout the whole process." Jade wrote quickly. Rosa had confirmed that the experiment could be repeated at home, and that all the materials were inexpensive and easy to find.

Jade turned to her own notes. She had interviewed Mr Davies, the head of science, the previous afternoon. He had told her that the science fair was one of the most important events of the school year. He had explained that it gave students the opportunity to investigate questions that genuinely interested them, rather than simply following instructions from a textbook. "He also said that Rosa's work was the best entry he had seen in five years," Jade said. "He told me that the evidence was clear and the method was excellent."

Ms Chen stopped by the newspaper room just before lunch. She read through the draft and asked whether they had included enough quotes from both sources. "She said it was important to represent two perspectives," Sam said, after Ms Chen had left the room. He made a note in his book. Ms Chen had also told them that the deadline for the current edition was four o'clock that afternoon. "She reminded us not to miss it," Sam added with a small smile. They both knew what happened when a deadline was missed — nothing was printed.

By three o'clock, the article was finished. Jade read it through one final time. It reported what Rosa had said about her process, what Mr Davies had explained about the purpose of the fair, and ended with a quote from Rosa, who had said that she hoped other students would feel inspired to carry out their own experiments. "This might be our best piece yet," Sam said. Jade pressed the button to send the file to the printer. Outside, the school day was almost over. Inside, the next edition was ready to go.

comprehension.question: What is the subject of Jade and Sam's new article?
options: An interview with Rosa about her plans for next year's science fair
The importance of the science fair in the school's learning programme
A report on how the school newspaper is written and published
How to grow crystals at home using household materials
comprehension.correctAnswer: The importance of the science fair in the school's learning programme
comprehension.explanation: The text says their article was 'about the importance of the science fair in the school's learning programme.'

comprehension.question: What did Rosa say was the most difficult part of her experiment?
options: Finding inexpensive materials that were easy to source
Keeping the temperature constant throughout the whole process
Photographing the crystals clearly for the poster
Persuading Mr Davies to include her entry in the competition
comprehension.correctAnswer: Keeping the temperature constant throughout the whole process
comprehension.explanation: Sam's notes record that Rosa said 'the most difficult part was controlling the temperature' and that 'it had to be kept constant throughout the whole process.'

comprehension.question: What did Mr Davies say about Rosa's entry?
options: That it showed potential but needed more detailed evidence
That it was a strong entry for a student of her age
That it was the best entry he had seen in five years
That it did not quite follow the correct scientific method
comprehension.correctAnswer: That it was the best entry he had seen in five years
comprehension.explanation: Jade's notes record that Mr Davies said 'Rosa's work was the best entry he had seen in five years' and that 'the evidence was clear and the method was excellent.'

comprehension.question: What did Ms Chen ask about the article?
options: Whether the headline was strong enough to attract readers
Whether they had included enough quotes from both sources
Whether they had spoken to Rosa's parents before publishing
Whether the article was short enough for the front page
comprehension.correctAnswer: Whether they had included enough quotes from both sources
comprehension.explanation: The text says Ms Chen 'asked whether they had included enough quotes from both sources.'

comprehension.question: What does Rosa say she hopes the article will achieve?
options: That her experiment will be repeated by the science department next year
That the school will invest more money in the annual science fair
That other students will feel inspired to carry out their own experiments
That the newspaper will start a regular science section
comprehension.correctAnswer: That other students will feel inspired to carry out their own experiments
comprehension.explanation: The article ends with Rosa's quote: 'she hoped other students would feel inspired to carry out their own experiments.'
